claim.content: In atomistic tight-binding calculations performed on relaxed twisted double bilayer graphene (TDBG) geometries, the low-energy moiré flat bands of relaxed TDBG are generally narrower than the corresponding low-energy flat bands of twisted bilayer graphene (TBG) for twist angles $\\theta>1.1^\\circ$, and the computed flat-band bandwidth for relaxed TDBG attains a local minimum near $\\theta\\approx1.25^\\circ$; this local minimum in bandwidth is identified as the first (primary) magic angle of TDBG in these calculations, and at $\\theta\\approx1.25^\\circ$ the TDBG low-energy flat-band bandwidth can be more than a factor of two smaller than the TBG flat-band bandwidth at the same twist angle.

conclusion.content: In atomistic tight-binding calculations performed on relaxed twisted double bilayer graphene (TDBG) geometries, the low-energy moiré flat bands of relaxed TDBG are generally narrower than the corresponding low-energy flat bands of twisted bilayer graphene (TBG) for twist angles $\\theta>1.1^\\circ$, and the computed flat-band bandwidth for relaxed TDBG attains a local minimum near $\\theta\\approx1.25^\\circ$; this local minimum in bandwidth is identified as the first (primary) magic angle of TDBG in these calculations, and at $\\theta\\approx1.25^\\circ$ the TDBG low-energy flat-band bandwidth can be more than a factor of two smaller than the TBG flat-band bandwidth at the same twist angle.

premises.content: Define the first (primary) magic angle of twisted double bilayer graphene (TDBG) in atomistic calculations as the twist angle $\\theta$ at which the computed low-energy flat-band bandwidth attains a local minimum on relaxed geometries; the weak point is the assumption that the reported local minimum near $\\theta\\approx1.25^\\circ$ is a robust, model-independent identification of a magic angle, meaning that reasonable variations in tight-binding hopping parameters, in the interlayer-potential parametrization, in the inclusion of further-neighbor electronic hoppings, or in the details of the structural relaxation model (for example, inclusion of anharmonic elastic terms or extension of the force-constant range beyond fourth nearest neighbors) would not substantially shift, remove, or change the physical significance of this local bandwidth minimum as a "magic angle."

steps.reasoning: Starting point (use upstream result as known): the relaxed TDBG atomic structures and their lack of inversion symmetry are taken as established from the upstream conclusion.

Define the quantity of interest and method: define "flat-band bandwidth" (the "bandwidth" is the energy spread of a selected low-energy moiré band or group of bands across the moiré Brillouin zone); the authors compute the bandwidths of low-energy moiré flat bands for both twisted bilayer graphene (TBG) and twisted double bilayer graphene (TDBG) using the atomistic tight-binding calculations on the relaxed structures, and compare them as a function of twist angle $\theta$.
[14]

Report the comparative trend for $\theta>1.1^\circ$: the computed bandwidths (plotted as a function of $\theta$) show that, for twist angles $\theta>1.1^\circ$, the low-energy moiré flat bands in relaxed TDBG are generally narrower (i.e., have smaller bandwidth) than those in TBG at the same twist angle; this trend is evident in the plot comparing TBG and TDBG bandwidths.
Fig. 2(e)

Identify the angle of minimum bandwidth and interpret it as a magic angle: the bandwidth in relaxed TDBG reaches a minimum near $\theta\approx1.25^\circ$ (the "minimum" refers to the plotted bandwidth versus $\theta$ having a local minimum at this angle); the authors interpret this angle $\theta\approx1.25^\circ$ as the first (primary) magic angle of TDBG in this system, in analogy with the notion of a magic angle where flat bands are maximally narrow.
Fig. 2(e)

Quantify the relative narrowing compared with TBG at the same angle: at the same twist angle $\theta\approx1.25^\circ$, the TDBG flat-band bandwidth is substantially smaller than the corresponding TBG bandwidth; the authors note that the TDBG flat bands can be more than two times narrower than TBG at $\theta=1.25^\circ$, which is supported by direct comparison of the band-structure plots at that angle where TDBG bands are nearly flat while TBG bands are visibly wider.
Fig. 2(c)
Fig. 2(d)

motivating_questions.content: Narrow bandwidth is a necessary condition for enhanced electronic correlations in moiré systems. While TBG exhibits discrete magic angles at which bands become ultra-narrow, it was unclear whether a four-layer twisted system would inherit similar magic-angle behavior, whether its primary magic angle would shift, or whether the layer-number degree of freedom could broaden the parameter window supporting narrow bands. Continuum estimates or extrapolations from bilayer results do not capture registry-dependent interlayer coupling or the impact of four-layer relaxation on the effective moiré tunneling amplitudes; thus the twist-angle dependence of bandwidths in actual relaxed TDBG remained unresolved, preventing assessment of how experimentally convenient the narrow-band regime is in multilayer stacks.
